items.question: What two sources of randomness define a random forest?
options: Sequential residual fitting and learning rate shrinkage
Bootstrap row sampling and random feature subsets at each split
Gradient descent on weights and dropout
items.answer: Bootstrap row sampling and random feature subsets at each split
items.explanation: Bagging randomizes rows; max_features randomizes which columns compete at each split.

items.question: For classification with p features, what is a common default for max_features?
items.explanation: Breiman's recipe and sklearn default use sqrt(p) for classification to decorrelate splits.

items.question: How is the final regression prediction formed?
options: Sum trees with learning rate η
Prediction from the deepest tree only
items.explanation: Regression forests aggregate by arithmetic mean across B trees.

items.question: Which statement best contrasts random forest and gradient boosting?
options: Both train trees sequentially on residuals
Forests parallelize bagged trees; boosting adds shallow trees to pseudo-residuals sequentially
Forests require feature scaling; boosting does not
Boosting averages votes; forests sum residuals
items.answer: Forests parallelize bagged trees; boosting adds shallow trees to pseudo-residuals sequentially
items.explanation: Parallel bagging vs sequential boosting is the core architectural difference.
